title: Booster Robotics launches Booster T2 humanoid on Nvidia Thor as an open embodied-AI platform
subtitle: The 75-degree-of-freedom bipedal robot ships with a 2,070 TFLOPS onboard compute stack and Booster Studio SDK — a bet that embodied-AI research needs a shared hardware platform, not one-off demos.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Booster Robotics unveiled Booster T2 on July 13 as its flagship humanoid platform for embodied-AI development. The bipedal robot has 75 degrees of freedom across legs, waist, arms, head, and end-effectors, and the Pro edition carries Nvidia's Thor chip for up to 2,070 TFLOPS of onboard compute — the highest listed on any shipping bipedal today. It ships with Booster Studio, an SDK that runs simulation training, policy development, sim-to-real transfer, and real-machine deployment in a single environment. The pitch is explicit: T2 is not a product for a consumer end-user, it is a research platform sold to labs that want to train and evaluate humanoid policies without building the robot themselves.

The launch matters because embodied-AI research is bottlenecked on hardware. Training a VLA (vision-language-action) model requires either a fleet of physical robots or a very good simulator paired with a robot you can eventually deploy on. Every research group building their own hardware from scratch spends 12 to 18 months on integration before they collect their first useful data. Booster T2, Unitree's G1, and Figure's Figure 02 are converging on the same idea — sell a common, capable body with an open SDK, and let the frontier of embodied-AI research shift to software. That is the same pattern that turned the Atari suite, MuJoCo, and later Habitat into standard benchmarks.

This lands in a busier July than usual for humanoids. Agility Robotics announced a 60,000-unit Fremont facility for its Digit humanoid last week. SoftBank, Yaskawa, and Nvidia demonstrated a deformable-object manipulation system on Nvidia's AI Data Center cloud. Meta's assured-robot-intelligence humanoid work continues under Superintelligence Labs. China's national embodied-AI program is fast-tracking humanoid deployment into factories and healthcare through a formal state initiative. The macro read is that embodied AI is transitioning from lab curiosity to industrial procurement — and the companies that provide the shared hardware, not the ones building bespoke demos, are winning attention.

For learners: the embodied-AI stack has the same three-layer structure as the LLM stack — hardware, foundation models, and applications — but the layers are still forming in real time. If you want to work in this space, learn a simulator that runs the same policies you would run on real hardware: MuJoCo, Isaac Sim, or Booster Studio. Being able to train a policy in sim, transfer it to a real body, and measure how badly it degrades is the practical version of the sim-to-real gap you will keep reading about. That skill translates directly into a hiring pipeline that is currently short on trained people and long on capital.
